skill: Punctuation — commas, semicolons, and clause boundaries
source: Derived from error patterns across the official ACT practice test (data/raw/act/)

mistakeCategories.name: Comma splice between two independent clauses
mistakeCategories.description: Student joins two complete sentences with only a comma.
mistakeCategories.studentExample: 'The results were clear, the team celebrated.'
mistakeCategories.synthesisCue: Offer a version that joins two independent clauses with just a comma.

mistakeCategories.name: Commas around essential (restrictive) information
mistakeCategories.description: Student sets off information that is necessary to the meaning, as if it were extra.
mistakeCategories.studentExample: 'Students, who study daily, pass.' (changes the meaning)
mistakeCategories.synthesisCue: Offer a version that wrongly wraps restrictive information in commas.

mistakeCategories.name: Semicolon where the second part isn't independent
mistakeCategories.description: Student uses a semicolon before a fragment or list intro.
mistakeCategories.synthesisCue: Offer a version using a semicolon before a dependent clause or fragment.

mistakeCategories.name: No punctuation where a pause is required
mistakeCategories.description: Student runs an introductory phrase or nonessential aside into the sentence with no comma.
mistakeCategories.studentExample: 'After the storm passed the crew returned.'
mistakeCategories.synthesisCue: Offer a version omitting the comma after an introductory phrase or around an aside.
